title: Cyber PIVOTT Act of 2025
plain_summary: The Cyber PIVOTT Act of 2025 creates a new government program to train people for cybersecurity jobs. The government will work with community colleges and technical schools to teach students the skills they need. Students who join the program get free tuition and all their school expenses paid for. In exchange, they must promise to work for the government for two years after they finish school. The program also includes real practice exercises, internships, and help finding jobs.
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Homeland Security and Governmental Affairs.
subjects: Government employee pay, benefits, personnel management

sections.title: Section 1 Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the Providing Individuals Various Opportunities for Technical Training to Build a Skills-Based Cyber Workforce Act of two thousand twenty five. It can also be called the Cyber PIVOTT Act of two thousand twenty five.

sections.title: Section 2 CISA Education and Training Programs and Resources
sections.text: This section adds new rules to existing homeland security law. It creates a new program called the PIVOTT Program that will help train people for cybersecurity jobs. The program defines many important terms. Armed Forces means the military services as defined in federal law. Community college means a two year school as defined in defense law. Cyber-relevant means areas that affect national security and computer safety, including things like artificial intelligence, quantum computing, and computer science. Director means the head of the Cybersecurity and Infrastructure Security Agency (CISA). Executive agency means government departments as defined in federal law. Institution of higher education means colleges and universities as defined in education law. The NICE Cybersecurity Workforce Framework is a government guide for cybersecurity jobs published in November two thousand twenty. Participating institution means community colleges, technical schools, or other two year schools that partner with CISA for this program. Program means the PIVOTT Program created by this law. Skills-based exercise means a hands-on training activity lasting at least one day that focuses on practice rather than studying. Technical school means a vocational school as defined in federal regulations. University-level educator means someone who teaches at a college or university. The Director must start the PIVOTT Program within one year after this law passes. The program will partner with community colleges, technical schools, and other two year schools to create training programs and help students get internships and federal jobs after graduation. Several types of students can join the program. This includes students who are about to start or just started a two year cybersecurity degree or similar certification at a partner school. It also includes people the Director identifies as ready to enroll in these programs, such as those changing careers, high school graduates, or entry-level workers. Students in shorter technical programs can also join if their training matches government cybersecurity job requirements. The government will pay full tuition and all related costs for students in the program. This includes academic fees, lab fees, travel, lodging, daily expense money, internship costs, online participation costs, certification test fees, and any other expenses the Director thinks are needed. This includes paying for one required in-person training exercise with travel, lodging, meals, and testing fees. Students who complete the program must work for two years in a cybersecurity job for a federal, state, local, tribal, or territorial government. However, this requirement does not apply to students who have already served in the military for two years, are currently in the military, or join the military in a cybersecurity role during or right after the program. Students who go straight into a four year degree program after finishing can delay their two year work requirement until after they get their four year degree. Students must finish the program within four years of starting, or follow their school's rules if those are different. Students facing extreme hardship can ask the Director for more time to finish. The Director will decide these requests case by case after talking with the student's school. Schools can participate in the program if they are already part of the National Centers of Academic Excellence in Cybersecurity program. Other schools can be approved by the Director if their courses match cybersecurity career paths and they have a cybersecurity clinic on campus. Students in the program must complete at least four hands-on training exercises. These can include lab work, competitions like hackathons and capture the flag events, online programming, tabletop exercises, industry workshops, or exercises in a box. The Director will work with schools to provide at least one exercise each semester. Students must complete at least one exercise in person. The Director will offer at least one in-person exercise to all program participants every two years. The Director can work with other federal agencies like the Department of Defense, FBI, NSA, and the Office of the National Cyber Director to provide these exercises. The Director can also work with non-government cybersecurity experts and clinics. Students who cannot complete a required exercise can propose a similar alternative that the Director must approve. The program requires internships as a core part of the training. The Director and schools will place students in approved cybersecurity internships with state, local, tribal, or territorial governments, critical infrastructure companies in rural areas or high-risk sectors, or federal agencies including the Regional Security Advisors program. Students who tell the Director or their school in writing that they want to work for the federal government beyond their two year requirement will get priority for federal internships that need security clearances. The Director will work with federal agencies to create internship programs for students who are already federal employees. The Director will start the security clearance process for appropriate students at least one year before they finish the program. The Director must conduct outreach in each regional CISA office and provide information about the program to industry partners. The Director will work with industry experts to create an annual report on job skills that should be included in the training exercises. This report may include input from a committee of university teachers that the Director creates. The report must be sent to the Senate Committee on Homeland Security and Governmental Affairs and the House Committee on Homeland Security within one year of this law passing and every year after. The committee of university teachers is exempt from federal advisory committee rules. Each year, the Director and the National Cyber Director will host a voluntary federal job fair with representatives from agencies looking to hire cybersecurity workers. Information about this job fair will be posted on a CISA website. The job fair can be online or in person and must be hosted at least five partner schools. Students who complete the program get access to an online database of cybersecurity training resources and federal job information. The Director will create and update annually a list of cybersecurity certification programs from private companies, schools, nonprofits, and other organizations. The government may pay for up to three certifications and tests per student through vouchers, as long as the student finished the program and requests the voucher within ten years of completing it. The Director may select up to ten students per year who completed the program and worked for the federal government for at least seven years to receive scholarships for cybersecurity degree programs at National Centers of Academic Excellence in Cybersecurity schools. Scholarship amounts will be decided by the Director based on available funding. Students must repay their scholarships if they fail to maintain good grades, get dismissed for disciplinary reasons, withdraw before finishing, say they will not fulfill their work obligation, or fail to complete their required government service. The Director can exempt students from repaying if they join the military before finishing the program or if they try hard for two years to get a government job after finishing but are not offered one. Partner schools must agree to monitor whether scholarship students meet their work requirements after finishing the program. If a student fails to meet requirements before completing one year of their required government work, they must repay the full scholarship amount immediately or treat it as a loan. Loans will be handled like federal student loans with interest starting from when the scholarship was first given. The government or schools acting for the government will collect repayments. Schools can keep a percentage of repayments they collect to cover administrative costs. The percentage will be the same for all schools and set by the government. The government can waive or reduce repayment requirements if paying would be impossible or cause extreme hardship. The Director and schools should try to enroll at least two hundred fifty students in the first full school year starting one year after this law passes, depending on available funding. Starting with the second year, they should try to double enrollment each year until reaching one thousand students per year. Within ninety days of this law passing, the Director and schools must create a plan to enroll at least ten thousand students per year within ten years of starting the program. The Director must brief Congress about this plan. If enrollment goals are not met in any year, the Director must brief Congress within thirty days after that school year ends. This section also updates the table of contents in the Homeland Security Act to include the new CISA education section. Within ninety days of this law passing, the CISA Director must submit a review of current CISA education and training programs to Congress. The review should evaluate the cost, reach, and demand for these programs and identify any resource gaps. The Secretary of Homeland Security must submit a report to Congress about current support for the CyberCorps Scholarship for Service Program and opportunities to provide additional funding through existing Department of Homeland Security programs.
